co_catalyst.name: Light-harvesting cofactor (MTHF/8-HDF/FMN)
co_catalyst.role: Absorbs light energy and transfers to FADH-

mechanistic_details.key_intermediate: Excited FADH- transfers electron to CPD, splitting cyclobutane ring

chemistry.scope_summary: Photoreactivation efficiently repairs UV-induced cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers in DNA using photolyase enzymes. The process involves light-harvesting cofactors (MTHF, 8-HDF, or FMN) that absorb visible/blue light and transfer energy to the catalytic FADH- cofactor, which then transfers an electron to the CPD lesion, splitting the cyclobutane ring within 0.6 nanoseconds and restoring the original pyrimidine bases.

figures.caption: Figure  1: DNA damage and maintenance. Genomic lesions produced by various DNA damaging agents trigger several speciﬁc repair machinery to conserve the genomic integrity. In case of severe damage and/or failure of repair mechanisms, cells undergo apoptosis or induce a complex series of phenotypic changes, that is, SOS response. Sometimes the potentiality of lesions in the genome is mitigated by a

Figure  2: Structures of DNA duplexes showing the presence of lesions (in green) such as CPD (a), 6-4PP (b), and 6-4 Dewar dimer (c). Hydrogen atoms are not shown, prepared from PDB entries 1TTD [ 24 ], 1CFL [ 25 ], and 1QKG [ 26 ] using PyMol. (version 1.1r1) [ 27 ].

notes: This is a biochemistry review paper about DNA repair mechanisms, not a synthetic organic chemistry paper with batch-to-flow translation. The paper focuses on photoreactivation of DNA lesions using photolyase enzymes rather than synthetic flow chemistry optimization. No quantitative synthetic chemistry data, optimization tables, or flow reactor information is available.
